primary_diagnosis.name: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome (suspected) presenting as paroxysmal AV re-entrant tachycardia (AVRT), spontaneously resolved

pathophysiology_summary: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome is caused by a congenital accessory atrioventricular pathway (bundle of Kent) that conducts electrical impulses between atria and ventricles outside the AV node. At baseline this produces ventricular pre-excitation (short PR interval, delta wave, widened QRS on the resting 12-lead ECG). The accessory pathway allows a re-entrant circuit to form, producing paroxysmal AV re-entrant tachycardia (AVRT): a sudden-onset, sudden-offset, rapid REGULAR narrow- or wide-complex tachycardia that can cause presyncope from reduced diastolic filling. The dominant danger is not the AVRT itself but pre-excited atrial fibrillation: if AF develops, rapid antegrade conduction down the accessory pathway can produce very fast ventricular rates and degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death. This is why AV-nodal blocking agents (verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, and to a lesser extent beta-blockers) are hazardous in pre-excited AF — they can preferentially favour accessory-pathway conduction. Definitive characterisation requires a resting 12-lead ECG to demonstrate pre-excitation, with electrophysiology study and catheter ablation as potentially curative.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden switch-like onset and offset of a rapid regular tachycardia with presyncope in a young healthy person is classic for a re-entrant SVT.
The patient is highly anxious and asks if it is panic — but panic-driven sinus tachycardia builds and settles gradually and does not switch off abruptly or cause true presyncope.

A cause of paroxysmal palpitations, but AF is typically irregularly irregular; the patient describes a perfectly regular rhythm. (Pre-excited AF remains the feared complication of the underlying pathway.)

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, a re-entrant SVT should already lead over panic despite the patient's anxious framing; anchoring on panic here is the key error.

differential.diagnosis: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (AVRT / accessory pathway, likely WPW)
differential.evidence_basis: Regular rhythm, abrupt onset/offset, presyncope, recurrence three times this year, structurally normal young heart — the accessory-pathway story consolidates.
Regularity, switch-like offset and objective presyncope are not features of panic; excluded as the primary explanation.
Regular character argues against ordinary AF, but pre-excited AF must be kept in mind as the dangerous mechanism and reason to avoid AV-nodal blockers.

A structural cause of exertional/arrhythmic symptoms and sudden death in the young — kept on the list until the ECG and (per source) a normal cardiac exam and preserved exercise tolerance make it unlikely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Focused history should collapse panic and elevate the accessory-pathway SVT while retaining structural disease as important-not-to-miss pending the ECG.

differential.diagnosis: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome (pre-excitation confirmed on resting 12-lead ECG)
differential.evidence_basis: A short PR interval with a delta wave and widened QRS on the resting ECG is the pathognomonic pre-excitation pattern; confirmation fundamentally changes emergency management of future episodes.
The other common cause of regular paroxysmal SVT; distinguished only when the ECG shows no delta wave / pre-excitation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The 12-lead ECG is the pivot: the whole point of the urgent referral is to look for the delta wave, since it changes future acute management.

differential.diagnosis: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome, suspected — paroxysmal AVRT, currently in sinus rhythm
differential.evidence_basis: Classic re-entrant SVT semiology in a structurally normal young heart; diagnosis cannot be confirmed remotely and rests on the resting 12-lead ECG and cardiology/EPS work-up.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm WPW; the correct end-state is a high-suspicion provisional impression with an urgent ECG arranged and conservative safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Presyncope during a high-rate tachyarrhythmia (haemodynamic compromise)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Near-syncope during the arrhythmia signals that the rate compromises cardiac output; the next episode could cause true syncope or worse.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissed as anxiety, the patient is discharged without the ECG and without safety-netting, missing an arrhythmia that can be life-threatening.

red_flags.description: Recurrent paroxysmal SVT in a young, structurally normal heart — suspected accessory pathway (WPW)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An accessory pathway carries a small but real risk of pre-excited atrial fibrillation degenerating into ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death; it mandates an urgent 12-lead ECG and cardiology review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to characterise the pathway leaves the patient exposed to a preventable sudden-death mechanism and to hazardous AV-nodal-blocker prescribing.

red_flags.description: Chest tightness and breathlessness during the high-rate episode
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ischaemic-type symptoms during a very fast rate indicate the arrhythmia is not haemodynamically benign, even though it resolved.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-weighting these symptoms supports false reassurance and under-triage.

red_flags.description: Sustained arrhythmia that does not self-terminate, or true loss of consciousness (not present today, screened)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Today's episode self-terminated and the patient did not actually lose consciousness; a future sustained episode or genuine syncope is an immediate emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If the patient is not told the threshold for calling an ambulance, a future sustained pre-excited tachyarrhythmia could be fatal.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient is young, very anxious, and explicitly floats 'panic attacks'. Accepting that frame ignores the switch-like onset/offset, the regular rate and the objective presyncope that mark a re-entrant tachyarrhythmia.

The patient feels completely well and the episode resolved. Treating 'stable now' as 'benign' misses that the resting ECG (and the accessory-pathway risk) is exactly what needs urgent characterisation.

Reflexively suggesting a rate-controlling agent (verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, or a beta-blocker) for 'palpitations' before the pathway is characterised can be dangerous if the patient develops pre-excited AF.

Trying to 'diagnose WPW' over chat: pre-excitation can only be shown on a resting 12-lead ECG, which telehealth cannot perform — the correct output is a provisional impression plus an urgent in-person ECG.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Resting 12-lead ECG features — short PR interval, delta wave, widened QRS (pre-excitation)
Current blood pressure, precise heart rate and rhythm confirmation
Cardiac auscultation for murmurs / structural disease
Whether an episode is ongoing pre-excited atrial fibrillation versus AVRT
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a likely paroxysmal re-entrant SVT with accessory-pathway (WPW) features, arrange an URGENT same-day / next-morning in-person 12-lead ECG and GP review with cardiology/EPS referral, teach a modified Valsalva for future episodes, and give firm 000 safety-netting for a sustained episode, true syncope or severe chest pain.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The risk is not over-calling WPW but UNDER-acting: reassuring the anxious patient that it is panic and discharging without an urgent ECG, or recommending an AV-nodal-blocking drug remotely.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Source note CDV-050 clinician reasoning (paroxysmal SVT / suspected WPW pathway)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Urgent resting 12-lead ECG to screen for pre-excitation (short PR, delta wave); modified Valsalva for acute episodes; cardiology referral for electrophysiology study; eliminate stimulant intake.
